items.q: What does 'participant observation' require the anthropologist to do?
items.correct: Take part in the community's daily life while observing and recording it.
items.misconception: Simply watch from a distance without any involvement.
items.random_wrong: Hand out questionnaires and then leave immediately.
items.why: Students confuse participant observation with detached observation, missing the active immersion in everyday life.

items.q: What is the difference between an emic and an etic account?
items.correct: An emic account uses the insiders' own concepts; an etic account uses the observer's analytical categories.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Emic refers to economics and etic to ethics.
items.why: Learners treat the two terms as interchangeable rather than as contrasting insider and outsider viewpoints.

items.correct: Because the researcher's own background and presence shape the data and its interpretation.
items.misconception: Because it makes research completely objective and bias-free.
items.random_wrong: Because it speeds up data collection.
items.why: Students wrongly think reflexivity removes bias, when its purpose is to acknowledge and examine the researcher's influence.

items.q: The central data-gathering method of social and cultural anthropology is:
items.why: Correct answer: Participant observation. “The laboratory experiment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An 'emic' account describes a culture using:
items.correct: The insiders' own concepts and meanings
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The insiders' own concepts and meanings. “The observer's scientific categories” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When an anthropologist reflects on how their own presence affects the community studied, they are practising:
items.why: It's Reflexivity. “Generalisation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Living among a community for a prolonged period
items.why: It's Living among a community for a prolonged period. “Conducting a single brief interview” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
